title: Chapter 1 — Make a cannon move
hero.title: Make a cannon move
hero.lead: We lay the foundations: how an image “comes to life”, how a cannonball follows a curve, and how we wrap it all up to share it.
s1.h2: The game loop: 60 frames per second
s1.lead: A game is like a cartoon: a sequence of still images shown very fast. On screen, it gives the illusion of movement.
s1.p1: About 60 times per second, the browser calls a function we name update. On each call, we move the objects a little, then redraw. That's the game loop.
s1.fig.cap: Listen to the keyboard → compute the movements → draw. And we start over, 60×/second.
s1.code.c1: // delta = time elapsed since the last frame (in milliseconds)
s1.callout: Why dt? Not all computers run at the same speed. By multiplying the movements by the elapsed time, the game runs at the same speed everywhere. Clever!
s2.p1: We split the game into scenes, a bit like the rooms of a house: the title screen, the settings screen, the match itself. We move from one to another.
s2.li1: 🏁 Boot — gets the game ready, the “press to play” screen.
s2.li2: ⚙️ Setup — we pick the names and the number of rounds.
s2.p2: Phaser handles this on its own: we give it the list, and we say “go to the Game scene”.
s3.p1: When the cannon fires, the ball goes in a straight line… then gravity pulls it down, and the wind pushes it sideways. The result is a nice curve: a parabola.
s3.p2: We store two numbers for the ball: its horizontal speed vx and its vertical speed vy. On each frame, we update them:
s3.callout.nerd: For the curious. Really, it's the same formula as in physics class (acceleration). You don't need to know it to play: the computer just adds up tiny steps, many times per second. Lots of tiny steps ≈ a beautiful curve.
s3.callout.try: Try it! Set the angle, the power and the wind, then fire. Can you manage to hit the red target on the right?
s3.sim.title: Shot simulator
s3.sim.hint: Negative wind = to the left, positive = to the right. The target moves on every hit!
s4.p1: My game works on my computer… but will it work on yours? Often, a program needs specific tools (this version of that software). If they're missing, it crashes.
s4.p2: Docker fixes that: we put the game and all its tools into a “box” (a container). This box works the same everywhere: on your PC, on a server, in the cloud. A bit like a shipping container: whatever the ship, the box is always the same.
s4.fig.cap: We build once, we get a box, and it runs identically everywhere.
s4.code.c3: # 2) serve them with a small web server
s4.callout: Remember. Batch 1 = a game that runs, inside a Docker box ready to be shared. Not pretty yet: that's the job of chapter 2!
